summary:
Bitumen (natural asphalt) was one of the earliest adhesive and waterproofing materials used by human communities, with evidence for its deliberate application in construction and tool hafting in Mesopotamia dating to around 6500–6000 BCE. Natural bitumen seeps in the region of modern Iraq and Iran provided a ready supply of the material, and communities at sites such as early Mesopotamian settlements used it to waterproof baskets and vessels, fix handles to tools, and seal reed structures.

Bitumen's versatility made it a valuable trade commodity as well as a practical building material. Its use foreshadows the large-scale application of the substance in later Mesopotamian urban construction, where bitumen would be used to mortar brickwork and line canals. The recognition of bitumen's adhesive and waterproofing properties demonstrates the empirical material knowledge that early Mesopotamian communities accumulated through sustained observation of their natural environment.
